data.name: Moroccan Lemon-Cumin Couscous With Raisins
data.description: A light, fluffy North African-inspired side dish where the warm, earthy notes of cumin perfectly balance the bright citrus of lemon and the subtle sweetness of plump raisins.

freezer.notes: Freeze in a sealed freezer bag, removing as much air as possible.

storage.reheating: Microwave with a splash of water, or gently warm on the stovetop over low heat.

instructions.text: Place the saucepan over medium heat. Add the olive oil and ground cumin, cooking for 30 seconds until the spice becomes highly fragrant.

doneness_cues.visual: Oil bubbles slightly around the spice
doneness_cues.tactile: Aroma becomes deeply earthy and noticeable

instructions.text: Pour the vegetable broth, kosher salt, and golden raisins into the saucepan. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil at 100°C/212°F.

doneness_cues.visual: Large, rapid bubbles breaking the surface of the liquid

instructions.text: Remove the saucepan entirely from the heat. Pour in the dry couscous and stir once to ensure all granules are evenly submerged. Cover tightly with the lid.
Let the covered saucepan sit undisturbed off the heat for exactly 5 minutes so the couscous can absorb the broth and plump the raisins.
Remove the lid and use a fork to gently scrape and fluff the couscous, breaking up any clumps and separating the grains.

doneness_cues.visual: Grains appear distinct and light, not clumped together

instructions.text: Pour the lemon juice, lemon zest, and chopped fresh parsley over the fluffed couscous. Fold gently until the finishing ingredients are evenly distributed throughout the dish.

troubleshooting.symptom: Couscous is clumpy and sticky instead of light and separated
troubleshooting.likely_cause: The couscous absorbed too much liquid, or was compressed with a spoon instead of fluffed with a fork
troubleshooting.prevention: Measure the liquid and dry couscous precisely by weight, and only use a fork for the fluffing step
troubleshooting.fix: Drizzle with 5ml of additional olive oil and gently scrape with the tines of a fork to coax the grains apart

troubleshooting.symptom: Dish tastes flat or lacks depth
troubleshooting.likely_cause: Spices were not bloomed properly, or the lemon juice evaporated due to being added while too hot
troubleshooting.prevention: Ensure the cumin sizzles in the oil at the beginning, and wait until fluffing to add the fresh citrus
troubleshooting.fix: Add an extra pinch of kosher salt and another squeeze of fresh lemon juice just before serving

chef_notes: Blooming the cumin in warm oil extracts its fat-soluble flavor compounds, creating a profoundly richer base than simply stirring raw spice into the cooking liquid.
Always use a fork to fluff couscous. A spoon will compress the delicate steamed granules against the side of the pot, causing starch release and immediate clumping.
For extra dimension in a festive setting, soak the raisins in a splash of warm water with a drop of orange blossom water for 10 minutes before adding them to the cooking broth.
Adding the fresh lemon juice at the very end preserves its bright, volatile aromatic compounds which would otherwise be destroyed by boiling temperatures.
data.cultural_context: Couscous is the foundational carbohydrate of North African cuisine, traditionally steamed multiple times over a simmering stew in a special pot called a couscoussier. While this recipe utilizes quick-cooking couscous for modern weekday convenience, it honors the classic Moroccan flavor architecture of balancing savory, earthy spices with bright citrus and sweet dried fruits.
